It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Explaining Indonesian, Chinese, and United States support for legality compliance of internationally traded products Benjamin Cashore and Michael W. Stone School of Forestry and Environmental Studies, Yale University, New Haven, CT, USA Abstract The purpose of this paper is to shed light on the emergence and institutionalization of a unique form of transnational business governance (TBG): legality veriﬁcation (LV) systems, which track products along global supply chains. Instead of imposing wide ranging global standards commonly applied through “gold standard” certiﬁcation systems, LV helps domestic governments enforce their own laws. Three processes help explain why Indonesia (as a producer), China (as a manufacturer) and the United States (as a consumer) moved, in the 2000s, from lukewarm interest or opposition to formalized support: how triggers enabled by economic interdependence are pulled; how ceding of instrument/process state sovereignty may reinforce substantive sovereignty; and historical institutional processes that shift organizational interests and problem deﬁnitions. Looking backward, the empirical results are consistent with a “Delaware effect” in which economic globalization is asserted to lead to a “ratcheting down” of environmental and social standards. Looking forward, we theorize about, and identify the conditions through which, widespread coalitions in support of the institution of LV, may pave the way for a “ratcheting up” process that is consistent with, but nuances, Vogel’s “California effect.” Keywords: California effect, legality veriﬁcation, private governance, supply chains, transnational business regulation. 1. Introduction A key challenge facing students of regulation, in the global era, is to better understand what types of policy initiatives might help ameliorate enduring environmental and social challenges in a world characterized by the increasing fragmentation of governance, authority, and institutions (Bernstein et al. 2011). There is arguably no greater illustration of the importance of this task than the case of tropical deforestation and degradation, where despite 30 years of international, regional, and domestic policy initiatives, ongoing resource deterioration continues to vex prac- titioners, as well as environmental governance and regulation scholars. The purpose of this paper is to analyze the synergistic potential and pitfalls of one of the latest global policy initiatives designed to promote environmental and social stewardship in the forest sector: the case of “legality veriﬁcation” (LV). Champions of this approach are turning to what Eberlein et al. These efforts are designed to trigger what Eberlein et al. term “transnational business governance interaction” (TBGI) (Eberlein et al. 2014) because the focus is on compliance with domestic laws and policies. These initiatives are situated within a complex myriad of public and private governance arrangements (Biermann et al. 2009). To date, most scholars have focused on particular insti- tutional arenas, such as international regimes (Keohane & Victor 2011) governing speciﬁc problems (Raustiala & Victor 2004; Rayner et al. 2011), domestic policy interactions in which “basket” approaches (Gunningham et al. 1998; del Río 2010; Oikonomou et al. 2010) are applied in an effort to improve behavioral impacts, or the role of international pathways in shaping domestic policy choices (Simmons 2009; Bernstein et al. 2011). While there is growing interest in understanding how different institutional settings interact, few studies, as Eberlein et al. note in this volume, have probed deeply into the causal mechanisms that might foster their institutionalization (Gehring & Oberthür 2009). As a result, little is known about the uptake of LV as a form of TBG, nor its TBGI potential, which draws on global supply chains whose producers, manufacturers, and consumers cut across very different country-level settings. This paper addresses this gap by asking two related questions: What explains historical and current support for LV as TBG? What are the implications of these ﬁndings for understanding the evolutionary potential of TBG and TBGI? Attention to the ﬁrst question reveals an empirical puzzle: a decade ago, support for the TBG concept of LV in Indonesia (a producing country), China (a manufacturer), and the United States (US) (an importer/consumer) was lukewarm at best. Yet as of 2012, all three national governments introduced formalized policies and/or legislation in support of LV. What explains these domestic policy changes in support of TBG? Drawing deductively on existing theories of the emergence of, and support for, transnational regulation (Vogel 1995; Bernstein & Cashore 2000, 2012; Cashore et al. 2004), as well as inductively from our empirical research, we argue that scholars revisit, and nuance, the role of three factors the literature has found to have some type of causal inﬂuence: economic (inter) dependence; the norm of sov- ereignty; and “historical institutionalist” processes in each country that shape the role, approach, and organizational capacity of government administrative agencies, business, and societal interests. In particular, we argue that while economic integration is a prerequisite, its existence does not correlate with support for TBG until triggers situated within global supply chains are pulled; that sovereignty over substantive policy outcomes may be reinforced by ceding sovereignty over instrument choice; and that domestic support occurs when historical events lead to coalitions that unite disparate business, government, and environmental actors. If borne out by further scrutiny, our analysis carries signiﬁcant implications for existing debates about the effects of increasing economic integration on environmental and social poli- cies. Many scholars argue that economic globalization can lead to a regulatory “race to the bottom” as countries lower environmental and social standards as a way to attract, or maintain, capital and investment (Daly 1993). This process has been labeled the “Delaware effect” to capture how this state’s “management friendly” regulatory and tax structure enticed ﬁrms to relocate from other states (Vogel & Kagan 2004). However, Vogel’s (1995) path-breaking research found that under certain conditions, economic integration could yield to a “ratchet up” of standards. In particular, Vogel highlighted the phenomenon in which ﬁrms operating in rela- tively highly regulated jurisdictions coalesce with environmental groups, creating “Bootleggers and Baptists” coalitions, whose purpose is to champion increased environmental and social Does California need Delaware? Vogel termed this phenomenon the “California effect,” to capture those cases in which highly regulated jurisdictions did not always produce capital ﬂight, but also capital preservation, by targeting sub-national states with weak environ- mental and social standards. While debates continue about whether the “Delaware effect” or the “California effect” better explain regulation in the global era, our analysis points to the need to integrate insights from both perspectives if we are to better understand institutionalization of TBG in general, and the divergence puzzle in our cases, in particular. As we elaborate in the conclusion, our analysis suggests that, unlike Vogel’s work on domestic and international public policies that already had policymaking authority, TBG scholars must assess how “upward” and “downward” pressures inﬂuence the institutionalization of TBG governance authority. Recognition of this leads to“two phases” of TBG emergence in which modest standards that embrace, rather than detract from sovereignty, are required to nurture domestic and global coalitions of “Bootleggers and Baptists” which, once entrenched, might generate higher standards while maintaining multi-stakeholder coalitions. The ﬁrst phase seems more consistent with “Delaware effect” processes while the second phase is more consistent with “California effect” logics. We illustrate and expand these arguments in the following analytical steps. Following this introduction, section two justiﬁes our research design and methods. Third, we highlight the key characteristics of LV within the context of TBG literature. Fourth, we document the shift in the 2000s from opposition to support for LV in each of our cases. Fifth, we review the strategic orientation of relevant governmental, economic, and societal organizations that appear to have played an intervening role in inﬂuencing domestic policy responses. Section six assesses the inﬂuences of market interdependence, sovereignty, and historical institutionalist processes in accounting for the changes in domestic policy responses. We conclude by assessing the impli- cations of our analysis for integrating non-linear evolutionary perspective, and the implications this approach raises for the potential of TBG evolution and interaction. 2. Research design and methods: Backwards and forwards The empirical portion of our paper draws on “process tracing” that includes paying attention to complex causal trajectories common within public policy (Hall 1986; Hacker 2002) (Heidenheimer et al. 1990) and comparative politics (Lijphart 1975). These projects draw on, but expand linear correlational relationships commonly applied in quantitative methods to include more nuanced qualitative analysis – processes often “black boxed” by quantitative research. We focus on identifying select domestic historical processes that appear to help explain current domestic support for TGB governance, and to which further scholarly scrutiny ought to be placed. The conclusion “applies forward” our analysis to reﬂect on implications for whether LV might either “lock-in” the “low bar” or alternatively “ratchet up” standards. As Patomäki (2006, p. 12) argues, social scientists do not just look at the past for its own sake, but because “[w]e are also interested in other possible and likely futures, and in determining the ways in which our actions and the actions of others contribute – sometimes via unintended effects and consequences – to making some of them real.” Such a reorientation, as Bernstein et al. (2000) argued over a decade ago, challenges most empirical social science focused on explaining empiri- cally measurable phenomenon that have already occurred, rather than theorizing about the contingent empirical pathways on which we are embarked, and through which strategic inter- ventions shape and inﬂuence pathways in often unpredictable ways.1 Does California need Delaware? Legality veriﬁcation as Transnational Business Governance (TBG) The idea of verifying whether production and manufacturing of consumer goods have followed the laws of the countries of origin has been gaining increasing interest by those championing improved apparel manufacturing facilities, and extractive processes within mining, ﬁsheries, and oil and gas production.Within forestry, such an interest has coalesced by those seeking to combat “illegal logging.” While no universally accepted deﬁnition exists, those groups addressing illegal logging share a common purpose in reducing illegal conversion of forests to other agriculture crops (deforestation); logging processes that ignore a range of governmental environmental and social policies, such as maintaining “no logging” buffer zones near streams and/or failure to comply with health and safety standards; and “theft” by logging on lands owned by others. For reasons we explain below, this deﬁnition has expanded to include not only “on the ground” practices, but also addresses those companies who contravene national log export bans, which have been introduced to encourage domestic manufacturing processes. Approaches to institutionalize LV have borrowed from private certiﬁcation or “Non-State Market Driven” (NSMD) global governance that was developed to bypass slow intergovernmen- tal processes; as well as domestic public policy focused “good forest governance” efforts that development agencies have been promoting within tropical countries for assisting them in addressing widespread governance challenges and domestic capacity building needs. Similar to “good forest governance” initiatives, LV efforts seek to improve the ability of developing country governments to enforce their own substantive policy requirements, explicitly reinforcing, rather than detracting from, national sovereignty. Like NSMD systems, LV’s mechanism for weeding out illegal logging is to track legal wood along global supply chains that cut across multiple jurisdictions. Likewise, third party auditing, rather than relying on traditional enforcement agencies, is emerging as a key mechanism to assure legal compliance (Table 1). As a result, sovereign governments are increasingly allowing non-domestic organizations to act as auditors for environmental compliance, mirroring trends in other arenas, such as election monitoring (Hyde 2009). For these reasons, LV carries the promise of helping realize the strategic self interest of a range of domestic organizations and actors: legal producers have a self-interest in support, as weeding out illegal supply is expected to increase prices; governments have a self-interest, as tax revenue is expected to increase, as well as improving general efforts to promote the“rule of law;” while environmental and social activists see important, albeit modest, improvements in on the ground behaviors governing environmental and social practices (Table 2). What is important for our story is that unlike certiﬁcation, support is now emerging not through consumer preferences for green products upon which NSMD systems largely rely, but through trade legislation by governments such as the US, the European Union (EU), and Australia that require importers of wood products to proactively ensure that their products were not derived from illegal sources.2 Table 1 Key features of legality veriﬁcation as transnational business governance Role of governments Sovereignty over policy substance reinforced, not challenged Policy scope Relatively modest Assurance Third party veriﬁcation Role of markets Tracking along supply chain Source: Cashore and Stone (2012), drawing on Cashore (2002); Cashore et al. The empirical puzzle: From opposition to support in Indonesia, China, and the United States Our review of domestic responses to transnational supply chain signals to support LV reveals variation across time within each of our country cases. In the 1990s, domestic opposition among most tropical developing countries was so strong that even the mention of “illegal logging” in international forums was viewed as inappropriate. Similarly, environmental groups and US business interests were quite cautious about the potential of LV to promote organizational objectives. Yet by 2012, the US, China, and Indonesia all passed legislation in support of and/or formalized LV. 4.1. Indonesia The ﬁrst formal recognition by the government of Indonesia of the need to address illegal logging came in 2002 when it initiated the Badan Revitalisasi Industri Kehutanan (BRIK, Indonesian Institute for the Revitalization of the Timber Industry), which was charged with monitoring and verifying that timber harvesting and production complied with relevant laws and regulations. To accomplish these objectives, BRIK issued certiﬁcates of legality (Eksportir Terdaftar Produk Industri Kehutanan or “ETPIK”) to export-oriented forest companies. However, many questioned whether this approach reinforced, rather than addressed, the problem of illegal logging by failing to create standardized approaches within Indonesia (Brown & Stolle 2009) and for creating an environmental in which “black market” certiﬁcates were easily produced (Colchester 2006). As a result, some wondered whether these initiatives were little more than paper exercises, with little intention to champion meaningful changes (Tacconi 2008). In 2003, the Indonesian government appeared, on paper, to step up its efforts by beginning to draft legislation that would create the Timber Legality Assurance System (TLAS), formally known by its Indonesian name, Sistem Veriﬁkasi Legalitas Kayu (SVLK). However, four years later, drafting was still not complete, leading many non-governmental organizations (NGOs) and international agencies to question Indonesia’s resolve. Yet by late 2007, legislation was ﬁnally submitted by the Indonesian negotiators to the Ministry of Forests for approval, and, in 2008 the SVLK was signed into law. In a departure from previous efforts that were criticized as limited, independent “third parties” were charged with auditing to assess whether managers and companies were in compliance with Indonesian law (Luttrell et al. 2011). In addition, civil society was empowered to provide independent monitoring and to submit objections (Gov- ernment of Indonesia and European Union Delegation to Indonesia 2011). China The ﬁrst formal approach from China to address LV followed the 2002 G8 Forestry Action Programme (Toyne et al. 2002), which subsequently produced the “Bali Action Plan” on illegal logging. The Chinese State Forestry Administration (SFA), which has responsibility for devel- oping domestic and international forest policy commitments, initiated three policy responses.3 First, the SFA declared that illegal logging was not a problem within China, but rather, concerned challenges in other countries, such as Indonesia. Second, despite evidence to the contrary (Chrystanto 2004), the SFA also provided assurances that its current systems ensured that tropical imports coming into China were compliant with the laws of the country of origin. Third, to assuage concerns, the SFA signed a 2002 memorandum of understanding (MOU) with Indonesia designed to reduce illegal exports through information exchanges (Governments of China and Indonesia 2002; Hurd & Simorangkir 2011). However, the MOU was generally viewed as a“paper exercise”with few observable effects in either country (Tacconi et al. 2004). No formal efforts were made by the SFA to change internal policies governing LV. However, beginning in 2008, China changed course by formalizing a nationwide approach to LV. The hallmark of this effort was the development of the Chinese National Timber LV System, ofﬁcially launched in December 2009. This system (implementation of which would continue throughout 2010), draws on China’s extensive permitting systems to establish chain of custody for all legally veriﬁed forest products within the Chinese forestry sector (Sun & Canby 2010).4 China also moved to strengthen the original Sino-Indonesian agreement by initiating a second MOU with more formalized commitments and actionable items, such as enhancing communi- cation around legal compliance challenges (Hurd 2011). The SFA also undertook two proactive efforts to assist Chinese operators: providing guidelines for domestic forest operations about how to meet legal requirements; and working with the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF) to develop joint “sustainable trade” guidelines focusing on how export-oriented companies might meet international LV demands.5 4.3. The United States While the problem deﬁnition of illegal logging was on the agenda of government ofﬁcials and environmental group leaders in the US in late 1990s and early 2000s (Gulbrandsen & Humphreys 2009), the US government failed to initiate any formal policies to ensure legality compliance at that time. However, by 2008, the US would leapfrog from laggard to leader by requiring, in formal legislation, a ban on the importation of illegally harvested timber. The codiﬁcation of this effort came in the form of an amendment to the Lacey Act, which expanded a 100-year-old law for preventing trafﬁcking in illegally obtained wildlife to include plants.6 The amendment was written broadly so that it could be applied to a range of forestry products including furniture, lumber, and pulp and paper products.7 In addition to forbidding the importation of illegal timber, the legislation contained two key provisions regarding enforcement. First, it required that those involved with the production and sale of wood products undertake “due care” to avoid importing illegal wood products. Second, lack of an “innocent owner” clause meant that accidentally importing illegal wood could not be used as a defense (Lacey Act 1900; Anderson 1995), but evidence of “due care” efforts could reduce non-compliance penalties. While enforcement and jurisprudence is still evolving, US agencies have indicated they will focus much of their attention on products whose materials were derived from the illegal harvesting of trees.8 (Table 3) Does California need Delaware? Indonesia LV came to the attention of a range of civil society and timber interests that had historically been divided over what objectives forestry governance instruments ought to address, as well as the most appropriate means for doing so.9 Forest-dependent communities and indigenous groups were concerned about timber concessions granted to industrial forest companies, while envi- ronmental groups had long battled industrial forest companies to support their preferred cer- tiﬁcation program, the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC). For these reasons, there was considerable skepticism among different interests about the potential of LV to overcome these differences. However, by the mid-2000s, industry, local communities, NGOs, indigenous organizations, and even auditors normally involved in forest certiﬁcation, all came to consider LV to be in their strategic interests. These groups went so far as to participate in the multi-stakeholder processes designed to inﬂuence the SVLK.10 Commu- nity groups came to see an opportunity for enhanced involvement in domestic forest delibera- tions. Industrial forest companies saw an opportunity in legality versus sustainability to meet, rather than ignore international demands. Likewise, government actors and agencies came to see LV as having the potential to help improve their anti-corruption efforts in general, and “good forest governance” initiatives in particular. 5.2. China In China, domestic timber industry interests were immediately clear in that they viewed LV as a protectionist non-tariff trade barrier designed to force costs onto Chinese industry.11 Partly as a result, the SFA was initially skeptical and focused most efforts on pacifying international con- cerns.12 However, by the mid to late 2000s, the SFA changed its strategic orientation considerably – deciding that some type of proactive response to international demands for LV might beneﬁt, rather than take away from their administrative authority. Likewise, in the late 2000s, industry associations changed their strategies, now arguing in favor of some type of formal response to international pressure. Similar changes occurred within the Chinese business community, who, in addition to lobbying the SFA to formally address international demands, also initiated its own efforts, such as creating online platforms for companies to jointly manage their supply chain legality assurance.13 5.3. The United States Like Indonesia and China, the strategic positions of key environmental, business, and govern- ment agencies concerning LV underwent signiﬁcant shifts in the US in the 2000s. Explaining domestic responses to TBG: From reluctance to formalization What explains the shift in public policy approaches towards LV? What intervening role did strategies of relevant government, business, and societal interests play? We now assess three traditional factors that have been used to account for domestic support of TBG institutions: economic (inter) dependence; the norm of “sovereignty;” and “historical institutionalist” processes. 6.1. Economic (inter) dependence Scholarship on TBG institutions has found that “economic dependence” on the targeted domes- tic sector (Drezner 2001; Drezner 2007) plays a key role in inﬂuencing whether, when, and how domestic support might occur.16 Attention is placed on assessments of domestic business inter- ests and/or government agencies on the strength of economic incentives (through either carrots or sticks) for possible support for TBG. Three additional insights emerged from attention to “economic interdependence” that can take place between consuming and producing countries. First, the country in which the source of international scrutiny arises can itself become the focus of the same complaint made by foreign competitors (Cashore et al. 2004).17 Second, when companies that are the targets of international scrutiny produce non-substitutable goods, they have greater potential to pacify economic pressures by shifting attention to other countries.18 Does California need Delaware? Several conclusions result from assessing the role of economic interdependence in explaining policy changes towards LV. First, Indonesia, China, and the US do share economic interdepen- dence over global forest supply chains. Indonesia has been a signiﬁcant exporter of timber, much of which is manufactured in China and exported to EU and US markets (White et al. 2006; FAOSTAT 2010). China itself is a substantial component of the global forestry sector, particularly since its accession to the World Trade Organization (WTO) (Gan 2004). The Chinese forestry sector is dependent on wood imports (Zhang & Gan 2007), the volume of which has been increasing following China’s 1999 decision to impose quotas on domestic logging (Xu et al. 2006). Meanwhile, US consumers “depend” on manufacturer products from these supply chains as they are often far less expensive than similar domestic products.19 Second, as economic interdependence has been a constant during the timeframe of our study, it cannot, by itself, explain changes from opposition to support of legality as a form of TBG. Instead, interdependence appears to act as a “prerequisite,” creating opportunities for leverage that can have varying degrees of inﬂuence. The empirical evidence from our cases appears consistent with this proposition. For instance, initial efforts to apply economic pres- sure were perceived by domestic economic interests in China and Indonesia as relatively weak. The 1998 G8 initiative to reduce the purchasing of illegal wood products, and the subsequent 2001 Bali ministerial summit in which Indonesia and the United Kingdom, US, and China signed MOU, contained no immediate economic consequences (Contreras- Hermosilla et al. 2007). Instead, they started the lengthy and ongoing EU Forest Law Enforce- ment, Governance and Trade (FLEGT) processes whose economic impacts would not be felt for years.20 The Indonesian government had, likewise, sent clear signals that it would require some type of meaningful economic incentive before formalizing its domestic LV efforts. In 2007, for example, Indonesia expressed to the European Commission (EC) that it would not sign a VPA until the EU imposed restrictions on imports from non-VPA countries.21 Indonesia’s approach of requiring clear economic incentives seemed to be reinforced following the failure of the European Parliament, in November of 2008, to pass the European Union Timber Regulation (EUTR) legislation giving preference to VPA countries.22 It was only after the European Parliament’s decision to reverse itself in November 201023 that Indonesia would ﬁnally sign the VPA in May 2011 (Samboh 2011). Owing to its role in the middle of the supply chain, the inﬂuence of international market incentives, while important, was initially less pronounced and fairly abstract. However, by the mid-2000s, China increasingly became the focus of NGO activities to increase economic pressure to support LV. Following estimates that as much as 40 percent of wood entering the EU from Southeast Asia was illegal – primarily from Indonesia – China, the WWF, and allies (Hirschberger 2008) worked to inﬂuence timber procurement policies by the UK, Denmark, Germany, France, and Spain (Sun & Canby 2010) to include legality components. Taken in its entirety, the approximately 20 percent of China’s total volume of timber exports to the EU represented by these countries (Brack & Saunders 2004; Brack 2005) was now subject to LV through EU member state procurement policies (Toyne et al. 2002). While these economic incentives certainly garnered the attention of Chinese export-oriented ﬁrms, it was arguably the passage of the Lacey Act amendment and the EUTR that provided the clear market signals necessary for Chinese timber industry ofﬁcials to assert pressure on the SFA Does California need Delaware?
