Reinforcing the importance of economic interdependence between producing and consum- ing countries, the US forest sector became increasingly concerned that the importation of illegal timber from tropical countries was deﬂating prices, hurting the competitive advantage of domestic ﬁrms. These worries coalesced when a study commissioned by the AF&PA estimated that illegal logging deﬂated international prices from seven to 11 percent (Seneca Creek Associates 2004), causing billions of dollars of losses to the US timber industry. It was only after this report was produced that the timber industry decided to jointly collaborate with the EIA. Hence, empirical observations from China, Indonesia, and the US do reinforce the impor- tance of economic interdependence as a prerequisite for shaping uptake, but that this must be linked with some type of concrete signal that economic losses may result for non-adoption (sticks) and beneﬁts for complying (carrots). However, while important, leveraging economic interdependence cannot, by itself, account for the shift from reluctance to formalization of TBG. First, though certainly more formalized, LV had not yet revealed any signiﬁcant economic incentives, which were either operating “at the margins,” or were promised to occur sometime in the future. Second, even if clear economic signals do materialize as LV is implemented, existing analysis shows that some countries, such as China, might suffer overall welfare losses (Gan et al. 2013). To gain a more nuanced and complete accounting, we turn to the role of sovereign Westphalian authority and the historical inﬂuences of institutional conﬁgurations in mediating and shaping evaluations for legality as TBG. 6.2. Sovereign Westphalian authority A second key factor that TGB scholars have identiﬁed, and which requires further scrutiny in our case, concerns the norm of sovereign Westphalian authority that underpins the international system, that is, the concept that national states have the authority, legitimacy, and responsibility to determine public policies within their own territories. Just how non-domestic pressures might inﬂuence, or be inﬂuenced by, the norm of sovereign authority (Bernstein & Cashore 2000, 2012) has been of longstanding interest within international relations, international political economy, and comparative public policy scholarship (Biersteker & Weber 1996; Strange 1996; Krasner 1999). The bulk of the scholarship on sovereignty and TBG focuses on the relative strength of non-domestic pressures vis-à-vis the desire of the country in question to maintain, as much as is possible, its sovereign authority (Krasner 1996). Given that the economic signals that emerged in the mid-2000s were, while important, rather abstract and/or focused on future incentives, we would expect sovereignty norms to“push back” on international demands. However, what seems to be at work in our cases is that the strength of sovereignty’s role in “fending off” outside pressures may depend on whether TBG is targeting substantive policy changes that affect some type of substantive “on the ground” policy require- ment (policy objectives or “ends) or the mechanisms to help achieve/enforce them (procedures or means). Does California need Delaware? B. Cashore and M. W. Stone © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 58 Page 11: These distinctions, long applied by comparative and domestic public policy scholars (Hall 1993; Howlett 2000) (Sabatier & Jenkins-Smith 1993; Auld et al. 2010), also seem useful for advancing TGB scholarship. Evidence from our cases reveals that that Indonesia, China, and US governments took action only once they had evaluated impacts on domestic substantive policies as minimal or non-existent. That is, greater support occurred when governments and strategists recognized that support of LV as a TBG policy mechanism might assist them in enforcing their own domestic policies and priorities, rather than challenge them. Recognition of this has led us to theorize that some government ofﬁcials and NGOs may even prefer that TGB approaches infringe on sovereignty over policy mechanisms, if doing so allows them to better realize their sovereign authority in creating domestic substantive policy objectives. Similarly, when TBG mechanisms do result in changes in domestic policies, but only on procedures – such as the imposition of multi-stakeholder inclusionary processes – rather than substance, the possibilities for domestic support seem to be higher. For instance, Indonesia’s decision to formalize LV in 2008 only came after it had assurances that international demands were not using LV as a “back door” to imposing international requirements on its legal forest regime. Indonesia made it clear, and TBG supporters accepted, that the standards of what constituted legality were still determined by the Ministry of Forestry.28 Yet, at the same time, the Indonesian government did acquiesce to international inﬂuences regarding critical enforcement procedures and multi-stakeholder processes.29 Before the SVLK, Indonesia’s program for auditing legal compliance, known as BRIK, forbade the participation of international auditors. However, recognition that inclusion of international auditors might assist Indonesia’s domestic“good governance”challenges, including addressing corruption challenges, while providing increased legitimacy to SVLK in international markets, led ofﬁcials to ultimately accept international auditors. Likewise, negotiations with the UK over its VPA process led Indonesia to accept and implement changes to the ways in which stakeholders were included in forest policy deliberations. In China, initial hesitation was not only owing to evaluations that LV was acting as trade barrier, but that it was also an unacceptable infringement on national sovereignty.30 This orien- tation echoed the Chinese government’s approach towards global forest certiﬁcation initiatives, which its 12th ﬁve-year plan identiﬁed as being in conﬂict with an “authoritative source of rules” (Buckingham 2012).31 For these reasons, even after international market signals were increased following the passage of the Lacey Act amendment and the EUTR, necessitating some kind of domestic response, Chinese ofﬁcials, still concerned about sovereignty challenges, were not quite ready to support global LV efforts.32 Only after Chinese industry and government ofﬁcials received in-person assurances from EU and US ofﬁcials that LV would not be used to attempt to change domestic Chinese forest management policies, was acceptance ﬁnally triggered. A pivotal event in this regard was when, soon after the Lacey Act amendment passed in 2008, US government ofﬁcials and members of US-based NGOs met with the SFA to outline their intentions and the approach of the Lacey Act. They took care to explain that their intention was not to initiate traditional protectionist trade intervention, but was instead designed to help countries, especially in the tropics, enforce their own laws.33 Sovereignty also played a key role in the US. The US timber industry was initially skeptical for two related reasons. First, ofﬁcials were concerned that supporting international LV efforts might challenge the sovereign right of the US government to determine domestic forests policies. Second, as a result, the US timber industry was concerned that environmental groups might also use LV as a “back door” to impose further regulatory restrictions on the US forest sector. It was Does California need Delaware? B. Cashore and M. W. Stone © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 59 Page 12: only after the EIA made it clear that it would not be using LV to inﬂuence or change US domestic forest management policies that the AF&PA and other industry players began negotiations in earnest. Though tentative at ﬁrst, leading timber industry ofﬁcials ultimately determined that the EIA was committed to an approach that would not challenge the sovereignty of the US government in general, and would not target US forest policies or practices speciﬁcally.34 So sensitive was the EIA to reinforcing its position on these sovereignty dynamics that it regularly met with industry to maintain communication and address outstanding issues. As one EIA ofﬁcial stated: “It’s not about good and bad and the speciﬁcs, it’s about the system and how it’s set up and how folks are incentivized and motivated. I think that helped us when we saw an opportunity to reach out, coming from the industry side.”35 Taken together, economic interdependence and sovereignty principles have played important roles in mediating domestic responses. A third overarching factor, however, also appears to carry explanatory power: “historical institutionalist” processes that shape the role, approach, and organizational capacity of government administrative agencies, business, and societal interests. 6.3. Historical institutionalist processes Whereas economic leverage and sovereignty have relatively straightforward and linear effects, a focus on “historical institutionalist” processes draws attention to the role of institutions and policies in shaping the structure and function of government, business, and societal organiza- tions, as well as complex feedback loops that reinforce, or alter, problem deﬁnitions, strategies, and norms (Skocpol 1995; Mahoney 2000). As these types of queries can ﬁll volumes (Gale 1998; Humphreys 2006; Singer 2009), our purpose is simply to highlight the importance of integrating these accounts into domestic policy decisions on TBG that otherwise might be biased toward recent events or ahistorical processes. Drawing on existing research on TBG, we place attention on the role of “macro” government institutional settings, the formal responsibility of adminis- trative agencies, and the organization of business and civil society interests (Cashore et al. 2004). We ﬁnd plausible evidence that shifts in strategic position from opposition to support for LV seem to be as result of “lagged”effects of complex, historical processes with feedback loops across government, business, and civil society. Our review illustrates the need for TBG scholars to engage in understanding and assessing these historical institutional processes to explain diver- gence in each of our three cases and, as a result, develop robust theories of TBG emergence that ahistorical/linear analysis are unable by themselves to provide. 6.3.1. Indonesia Attention to the role of historical institutionalist processes focuses attention on democratic and decentralization reforms that followed the fall of the Suharto regime in the 1990s.36 As part of these reformasi initiatives, greater responsibility over forestry decisions was granted to regional governments (Casson & Obidzinski 2007; Singer 2009).37 However, the federal Ministry of Forestry never fully supported these efforts,38 resulting in an institutional type of “tug of war” between the central and regional governments (Purwanto 2005). These conﬂicts created confu- sion, overlapping authority, and contested governance, leading to widespread inefﬁciency and implementation barriers that exacerbated key challenges associated with illegal logging, includ- ing unsustainable levels of logging alongside reduced afforestation and investment (Purwanto 2005).39 The aftermath of these trends was overcapacity of domestic forest producers and mills, resulting in widespread bankruptcy of smaller ﬁrms. The federal government’s initial response to these forest challenges was to “recentralize” forest management and control (Agrawal & Ribot Does California need Delaware? B. Cashore and M. W. Stone © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 60 Page 13: 1999; Singer 2009).40 The government also brought back export control restrictions (that the International Monetary Fund [IMF] had initially required as a condition of receiving ﬁnancing post-Suharto) in an effort to ensure more ﬁber for Indonesian mills, much of which was being sent to more lucrative markets abroad. Legislation was enacted in 2001 banning the export of logs, which was followed by a ban on sawnwood in 2004 (Chen 2006).41 However, higher prices in international markets meant that many loggers were ﬂouting the export ban (Chrystanto 2004), resulting in expanding the problem deﬁnition of illegal activity to include not only forest management practices, but also trade. As a result, Indonesia’s forest producers came to view international demands for LV as not simply creating potential constraints for timber production, but would help to enforce export bans, enabling increased supply for their manufacturing facilities.And, the increasingly concentrated Indonesian forest sector – including concessionaires and manufacturing ﬁrms – came to see their support of LV as having the potential to secure access to lucrative markets while reducing competition with trading in illegal forest products. Recognition of these historical dynamics helps explain why industry participated in the VPA process42 and why government agencies came to see LV as helping it focus, not only on supply challenges, but also demand. These same post-Suharto reforms sparked a broader set of changing problem deﬁnitions concerning responsible forest management and a growing role for civil society. One legacy of decentralization efforts was greater attention to forest-dependent communities, forest liveli- hoods, and the rights of indigenous communities. These shifts led to the promotion of domestic “good forest governance” initiatives in general, and to tenure rights in particular. Both efforts championed the notion that local peoples could better beneﬁt from, and participate in, the forest sector. The articulation of these interests and their increasing coordination and organization – something that was truncated under Suharto – was reinforced during the 2000s by a range of international development agencies and non-governmental interests, such as the Rights and Resources Initiative. The result was that LV came to be supported not only by industry players, but by communities who saw an opportunity to expand LV further to include legal rights of local peoples. 6.3.2.
